sentences.text: Yeah, Yeah.
I'm gonna go into '37, '38, '39.
Oh.
I don't think at my age-- at that time I was what, 14 years?
Less, you know, we're talking about '37, '24.
I was 13 years.
Nothing was a threat to me.
I wasn't afraid of anything.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went places in Poland before the war, which I wouldn't let my son go today.
We went-- for 10 cents, we took the streetcar out of town.
The streetcar took you for 10 cents out of town.
You were riding and-- and that little-- there was a little-- not a river, but a little lake.
It's not even a lake.
What would you call it?
You know, you know, little thing with water.
And we would walk maybe three, four or five kilometers to get to that.
And we went between the-- what is it called?
It's not corn.
You know, wheat and-- you know those things.
We call them corn in Europe, that bread is made of.
Wheat.
Wheat.
The wheat was taller than us.
And it was just a-- and we went through that, and-- and we weren't afraid of nobody.
There was nobody, and it was all 100% Christian country.
You know, maybe three or four or five boys, we-- we went there.
And we went to the water, and we swam, and we played around.
And today, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was maybe 10 years old or 11 years old.
There was not-- things don't happen here.
It was unheard of.
No, no, not where we lived.
I lived in a-- when I went to school, I was seven years old.
I couldn't speak one word of Polish.
Only Yiddish.
And-- and-- and they put down my name.
My mother took me over to the school the first day.
And the teacher could speak Yiddish, just like me.
Most of the teachers were Yiddish.
And they started out to explain that this is, uh-uh.
And in no time, I could read and speak.
Didn't take us, you know.
And-- and here, they talk about the schools are crowded.
We were 60 children in one class.
There was 10 desks, three, two at the desk.
And the discipline was so strong.
I couldn't come home and say to my father that they did something wrong to me.
He would hit me, too.
Not like here, they run down and have a fight with the teacher.
You wouldn't dare go into class with your hands in your pocket, or things like that.
I mean, it was different.

I-- I said to my wife many a time, you know, when I came over here, the first year, 1950, and I went to night school, a few times I went, and the first time I went in class and the teacher spoke to a Mexican guy and he came out.
She called him out, and he came out.
And he stood there--
And he stood there and held his hands in his pockets.
And I said-- I thought to myself, what-- what's going on here?

In-- in 1938-- I think it was in '38-- they-- Germany picked up the Jews, and they put them on the Polish border, the Jews, which were born in Poland and lived in Germany, though they were citizens of Germany.
And-- and Poland refused to take them in.
So they put them on the border land.
I don't remember what the name was.
And so the-- the Polish didn't let them in, and the Germans didn't let them back.
And they were sitting in no man's land out in the open.
That's when I became aware, because they started collecting money and things to send to those Jews.
After a while, Poland let them in.
And one of those people came to my house.
I don't know what he was to us.
He knew us or something.
And he explained how bad it got.
They took away everything, you know, that-- I was at home.
I listened to all that.
That's when I became aware.
Then they started out for Czechoslovakia.
First was Austria, but this didn't bother us.
But when it came to Slovakia, they gave it away.
They signed away.
I remember at the house, we talked about that, that we said that England and France took a country, which didn't belong to them, with which they have no border, and they gave it away.
How can this be possible that they can give away a-- a whole country?
And the Poles started to talk that they want colonies.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they did get a piece of Czechoslovakia, which they called [INAUDIBLE], which was on the other side of the Olza River.
The river was the border.
They got a strip from that.
And they wanted colonies, too, so they came on-- on-- on-- on the big square-- it must have been in '38 or something-- and they made demonstrations.
We want-- we want colonies.
We want, you know-- and they started out to organize us.
They took us from school and showed us how to cover up, let's say, a burning fire with-- with-- with sand instead of water, because the water makes it only worse.
We were-- we were still children.
They explained to us how to do about it.
Not that it made any impression on me, you know?
But-- What was that supposed to be for?
In case bombs are drops, you know, and-- and how to put out fires and things.
They showed us how to put on a mask.
We didn't have any masks yet.
And they-- they-- they showed us how to dig trenches like in a Z formation in the park.
So in case of attack, we would get into this as a protection.
And they were making air raids.
I mean, not air raids but training for air raids, you know, the sound of sirens and things.
So the air changed in Poland.
And they started blaming the-- the German Volksdeutsche to look after them so they don't do sabotage and other things.
So the atmosphere, the whole atmosphere became tense.
And it changed.
People tried to get out to go to other countries, but this was-- there was no country letting Jews in.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It-- it became-- people tried to get to Palestine, but they-- there was a very limited amount who could get, and-- and I imagine who could get out.
My father was very much against all that.
He was against leaving home.
He believed very much that a family stays together in the home.
And we were not doing bad.
It's not gonna happen, you know, it's gonna be all right.
It's not gonna be a war.
And then when war was inevitable, we believed it's gonna be over in six weeks, or in six months.
And the First World War, all right, we hungered and all that, but we survived.
We had no idea what Germany was, no conception, and no understanding what kind of power they were at that time.
Poland had a few planes, and they were flying around.
And then those-- somewhere those bi-wings or single wings.
They put a little plane, on-- on-- on-- on the main-- I had a picture of it.
I couldn't find it-- on the main-- on the main plaza, you know, the Independence Plaza.
And when I think back, it's laughable how this was gonna protect us.
And they brought out the--the-- the Army.
You know, the Army was horses, horse-drawn.
In 1938-- or it was in '39, the beginning of '39-- they mobilized.
Poland mobilized.
And my brother was called in, and my other brother-- two brothers, they were both called in.
My oldest brother Leibel was called in.
He was already in the reserve.
And Hershel, both was in the reserve.
Leibel was stationed someplace not far from Lodz, and my-- my younger brother, Hershel, was taken to Warsaw.
They had this fort.
It was called Modlin.
And this was supposed to protect the city of Warsaw.
Of course, it was-- it did-- it did in-- in-- in the First World War, but it didn't do it in the second war.
And then they were released again.
And it kind of went back to quiet.
The-- the Germans occupied Czechoslovakia.
And then they wanted a-- a corridor through the corridor.
Poland-- Germany, on top of north of Poland, had part of Poland going through Germany, into the ocean.
It was maybe a territory from 12 miles or something.
Now, the Germans wanted a corridor through those 12 miles going from western Germany onto the east of Germany, which was Prussia.
I'm coming down to that.
So when-- when the war-- in 1939-- when the Poles said they're not gonna give, and the English said they're gonna protect us, Poles said we're not gonna give nothing.
So on-- on Friday, September the 1st-- I think it was a Friday-- I went with my father to the-- to iron supply to buy some metal he needed.
It was long strip of metal, so he needed me to help him carry it home.
It was long, maybe 10 foot.
You know, I would take one end and he would take one end, and we'd walk home.
Being there, there was a few airplanes in the sky.
And they were so high, you couldn't make them out.
But they were planes, and people said those are our planes.
The war was already on.
This was maybe eight o'clock in the morning.
We didn't know.
We came home and we read the papers.
And my two brothers were already in the Army.
They were mobilized maybe a month before or something.
And that day, we heard the war was on.
The Germans are at war with-- with Poland.
The German declared war on Poland.
Next day was Saturday, and there was movement of the army, the Polish Army moving to the fronts, to out of town to-- on foot.
I went down to see this.
I-- all you could see is soldiers marching.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there was nothing, no shots, nothing.
The city was bombed maybe a couple times.
A house was hit, on the [INAUDIBLE] 67 or 66, and it was burned.
Other than that, there was nothing.
Outside, we knew the war was on.
People started hoarding food.
It was already hard to get, because the stores sold out in no time.
Nothing was delivered in-- in the very first days when the war was on.
But we in the city of Lodz did not have any direct involvement of the war.
The war was already on.
No, when did the direct involvement begin?
Direct involvement became maybe on Wednesday or something.
You could hear the cannons.
You could hear the shooting.
You could see planes flying, but they didn't do nothing.
And the city was taken without a shot in eight days into the war.
On-- on-- on a Friday night, it was either the 7th or the 8th.
I think it was the 7th, September the 7th.
People said that, on-- on the [INAUDIBLE] Square are-- are set up four machine guns with Germans.
The Polish Army disappeared.
The police disappeared off the streets.
And the square was from us maybe six, seven blocks.
Nobody went to-- to look.
It went by word of mouth.
But we knew it to be true because people-- you didn't have to go on the street.
You know, it went through alleys and backyards and all that.
So the German Army, on-- on Friday night, was already in Lodz, not maybe the Army, but the advance posts.
On Saturday morning, I woke up.
There was a rumble.
We lived from the main street a half a block.
So the German Army was going in.
And this was the street they had to take from Lodz going to Warsaw.
This was the main highway.
Of course where we lived, it was a street, but after, it was a highway.
And I went down to look.
And I came on the [INAUDIBLE] and there was the German Army marching.
And I have never seen anything like this in my life.
Here was an army where nobody marched.
Everybody was sitting either in a car or on a truck with mounted machine guns with double barrel, which I've never seen in my life, with cannons mounted on-- on-- on half tracks.
They even had steamrollers to straighten the street after the army marched.
They had field kitchens.
When they stopped, they served rice.
We used to believe that the Germans don't have enough food, because they put everything in armament.
They're starving.
Or they used to say the slogan, armament instead of body.
They had plenty of food.
The-- the Army looked clean, shaved, dressed, with cameras, taking pictures like tourists.
We were glum.
The Poles were glum.
There were some Germans who already had German flags.
They bought flowers for the German soldiers.
They already had the [INAUDIBLE] and the arm band.
And-- and some German flags were already flying from some buildings.
And that army marched all day long without interruption, from morning till late, till I don't know when.
Till I went to sleep, that was still-- you could still hear them marching.
The noise was there.
It wasn't marching by foot, it was marching by equipment, by-- by tanks and by trucks and by-- I don't know, you name it, they had.
I have never seen an army like this till I saw the American equipment.
It-- it changed instantly.
It changed up so the-- the-- the-- the fear preceded them.
I mean, I was a boy.
I didn't understand that much.
But that's the way-- when I think back, that's the way it was.
People were already afraid.
People already knew that nothing good, especially Jewish people, that nothing good came out of it.
And in-- in the same day, or the day next, there was already a German paper on the street with the-- it was the [INAUDIBLE].
That's what they named it.
And the big headlines were the Fuhrer [GERMAN], which means the-- the Fuhrer liberated us.
It was a German paper printed in German.
I could read that much.
I could make that out, though I couldn't read German.
But I could-- similar to Yiddish, you can-- you can get idea.
And in the beginning it wasn't all that bad, but it wasn't good.
Right away, they made certain restrictions.
The first-- the first-- they came in and it was September.
And it took four-- four weeks.
There was nothing going on in Lodz like war-wise.
There was no shooting, no nothing.
The Army moved on right away, you know?
Nothing was destroyed in Lodz.
Everything was standing the way it was.
And of course, it wasn't normal.
People didn't open up the stores and things like that.
Things were being sold behind the back.
And nobody knew the money is gonna be worth what, and so on.
So it wasn't normal.
Now before the Poles went out of Lodz the last army.
I'm going back two, three days, like maybe to a day before the war.
Not before the war, no.
It was already in the war.
It was maybe on a Wednesday.
So when the Polish Army moved out altogether, they told the population to come along, the-- the-- the men, you know, to come along.
We'll fight the Germans.
And so a lot of people, on-- on the thousands, not just Jews, I mean, Poles and Jews and all that, went along on that same highway where the Army came in later, like I just mentioned.
And my father didn't let us go.
He said no, you stay at home, and we don't run.
We have more chance of survival at home under your roof than going to who knows where.
So I'm not gonna go into the people who went, because I wasn't there.
Now, the war went on for-- till four weeks.
After four weeks, Poland capitulated.
They had the guarantee that the soldiers who were being taken in Warsaw will not be taken to POW camps.
They will be released on the spot.
They will be free to go home.
Now, my brother was in Warsaw.
We know that.
We knew that.
So my brother came home right away.
He came home--
My bother Leibel, my oldest brother.
He was hit in the nose by a shrapnel, by a piece of shrapnel.
It cut his nose, and they just tied it on with-- with-- whatever.
And-- and it healed, and he went home.
And then, after the war in Poland was over, which was four weeks, I don't remember how long, we received a letter from my other brother.
And he was in a POW camp in-- in a stalag.
I don't remember the number of the stalag.
They called it a stalag.
And at that time, they were not separated, the Jews from the Polish soldiers.
They were all-- and it didn't take long.
We had a couple letters, and then he came home.
Now this was in '39.
In-- in-- in October, it was the Jewish holidays.
So they announced that the Jews cannot pray on this holiday.
That was Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur.
So the temples were closed.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Temples were closed.
You weren't supposed to pray at home, either.
Of course, nobody could stop you from praying at home, but you had to hide, you know, not stay in the window and pray.
And so they dissolved the-- the Jewish committee, or the Kehillah, and on the 11th of November, which was Polish Independence Day, we woke up in the morning, and everybody was all excited.
They-- they torched the two last synagogues in the city.
First, there was an explosion.
They-- on the-- on the main square was a statue of Tadeusz Kosciuszko.
He was a revolutionary.
He got up against the revolution in-- I don't remember what year it was.
And he was a very famous hero in Poland.
So they blew up his statue.
And in the same day, the same night, they burned the-- the orthodox synagogue and they burned the reformed synagogue.
Well, here it wouldn't be-- here it would be conservative.
Over there, it was reform.
We called it the German synagogue, because we used to-- I don't know why we called it the German, but I-- I imagine because the Germans were so far more reformed than we were.
And this was a very, very big tragedy.
Of course, we didn't know what tragedies there is to follow.
So on the next day, I went down to the-- to Independence Square to see.
I was a boy.
They told me not to go, but I was a boy.
Who's going to bother a boy, you know.
So I went down, and they laid him out on the ground, and they put up a barrier-- not a barrier, but like a-- like a-- a string strung out around him.
And you could go around in a circle and see him, you know, how he was laid down there.
The statue of Kosciuszko, you know?
And the Poles were crying.
And the Jews didn't go to look.
Maybe some like me, you know, but generally, I don't think so.
And the synagogue, was there nothing to see.
It was burned out.
It was burned out, a skeleton, both synagogues.
The synagogues were-- were situated in very dense populated areas, but it was so done that it didn't affect the-- the other houses around it.
And this was already in November.
And then came winter, and they would talk about making a ghetto.
So I don't know exactly how it was decided, but we knew that there's gonna be a ghetto.
We also knew that the ghetto is gonna be where we live.
We lived in the poorer section of town, so naturally, that was gonna be the section of town they're gonna give to the Jews.
That section of town also contained the majority of the Jews to begin with.
I would say 60 or more percent of the whole Jewish population of the town lived in that section.
And the people started to move.
In the meantime, things happened like people were thrown out of apartments within five minutes' notice in the town, in the fancier parts of town, which the Germans moved in.
So there came a time-- well, this is hearsay.
Right.
So later, they came established that this-- those streets are gonna be the ghetto.
It was a big area.
It was a big area.
This-- I-- it-- it's a lot of streets.
I-- I have it in book, but I don't know.
Anyway, our-- our street was in ghetto, you know, almost in the heart of the ghetto.
So people started moving, and the Germans, you know, first, they requisitioned that you cannot take any money out of the bank.
You cannot put any money in the bank.
You cannot change any money in the bank.
You had to have a Pole go for you to the bank and change-- and change some of the money.
OK, we're gonna change the tapes now, while we're changing the money.
